explanation: Since x = 7 and 7 > 5 is true, the if body executes and prints "big"; the final println is outside the if block and always executes, printing "done". Distractor A forgets the unconditional statement; C and D misread the condition.

stem: Which relational operator in Java means "not equal to"?
explanation: `!=` returns true when its two operands are not equal; `==` tests for equality, `!` is the logical NOT operator applied to a boolean, and `<>` is not valid Java syntax.

10 % 3 equals 1, not 0, so the condition `a % b == 0` is false and the else branch executes, setting result to 2. Distractor B results from incorrectly assuming 10 is divisible by 3; A reflects the initial value that would remain if neither branch ran.

Without braces, only the first `println` is inside the if body; the second `println` is always executed regardless of the condition. Since n = 4 > 0 is true, "positive" prints, and then "not zero" also prints unconditionally. Distractor A is the intended but incorrect assumption a student with the missing-braces bug would make.

options.label: A
options.text: `new String("hello")` is not valid Java syntax

options.label: B
options.text: `==` compares object references, not content; `s1` and `s2` are two distinct objects in memory, so `s1 == s2` is `false`

options.label: C
options.text: The `else` keyword must appear on the same line as the closing brace of the `if` block

options.label: D
options.text: `"hello"` cannot be passed to the `String` constructor

explanation: Using `new String(...)` creates a new object on the heap each time; since s1 and s2 refer to different objects, `==` returns false even though they contain the same characters — `s1.equals(s2)` is the correct test for content equality. Options A, C, and D describe non-issues: all three constructs are valid Java.

score = 75: 75 >= 90 is false, 75 >= 80 is false, 75 >= 70 is true so the third branch assigns "C" and all remaining branches are skipped. Distractor B results from confusing the 80-threshold branch with the 70-threshold branch; D would require score < 70.

x = 8 > 5 is true so the outer if body runs; inside, y = 3 > 5 is false so the inner else executes, printing "B". Distractor A requires both conditions to be true; C requires the outer condition to be false; D is impossible because exactly one branch of each if-else runs.

options.label: C
options.text: An `ArithmeticException` is thrown because of division by zero

explanation: Short-circuit evaluation of `&&` means that once `x != 0` evaluates to false (since x is 0), the right operand `(10 / x)` is never evaluated — the division by zero never occurs — and the else branch executes, printing "no". Distractor C is the classic trap for students who forget that `&&` short-circuits on the first false operand.

Java's dangling-else rule pairs an else with the nearest unmatched if — here, the inner `if (q > 20)`. Since p = 5 > 3 is true, the inner if is reached; q = 10 > 20 is false, so the inner else executes and prints "Y". Distractor D is the common error caused by reading the indentation as though the else belongs to the outer if, which would mean the else only runs when p <= 3.

stem: Which of the following is equivalent to `!(a > 5 && b < 10)`?
explanation: By De Morgan's law, `!(P && Q)` equals `!P || !Q`; negating `a > 5` gives `a <= 5`, and negating `b < 10` gives `b >= 10`, so the result is `a <= 5 || b >= 10`. Distractor A incorrectly keeps `&&` instead of flipping to `||`; C negates the operator but leaves the operands un-negated; D uses strict inequalities where `<=` and `>=` are required.

All three if statements are independent (no else-if chain), so each is evaluated separately: `a > b` (6 > 4) is true so P prints; `a < c` (6 < 9) and `b > 2` (4 > 2) are both true so Q prints; `a == b` (6 == 4) is false so `!(false)` is true and R prints. Distractor B incorrectly assumes the second condition is false; A stops after the first.

stem: Which of the following expressions is equivalent to `!(x >= 0 || y != 5)`?
explanation: By De Morgan's law, `!(P || Q)` equals `!P && !Q`; negating `x >= 0` gives `x < 0`, and negating `y != 5` gives `y == 5`, yielding `x < 0 && y == 5`. Distractor A incorrectly uses `||` instead of `&&`; C omits negating the individual conditions entirely; D negates only the first operand.

options.label: C
options.text: `C` on one line, then `D` on the next line

explanation: The three if statements are independent — not an if-else-if chain — so every condition is evaluated separately. score=75 fails 75>=90, passes 75>=70 (prints "C"), and passes 75>=60 (prints "D"). Options A and B reflect the trap of thinking only the first matching branch executes (as in an if-else-if); option D reflects the mistake of thinking score>=90 is also satisfied.

Without braces, only `System.out.println("greater")` is inside the if body; `System.out.println("than three")` is unconditional and always runs. With n=2, the condition n>3 is false, so "greater" is skipped. "than three" and "done" both print. Option D assumes braces are implied by indentation; option A assumes both indented statements are guarded; option B misreads the condition as true.

a=8 satisfies a>5, so the outer if body executes. Inside, b=3 fails b>5, so the inner else branch runs and prints "only a big". Option A requires b>5 to be true; option C requires a>5 to be false (triggering the outer else); option D is impossible because a single if-else can execute at most one branch.

`new String(...)` explicitly allocates a new object, so `p` and `q` refer to distinct objects in memory — `p == q` is false (different references). `p == "Java"` is also false because `p` does not point to the string-pool literal. `p.equals(q)` compares character content and returns true since both hold "Java". Option D would require equals to return false, which it does not.

Short-circuit evaluation of `&&` means the right operand `word.length() > 2` is never evaluated once `word != null` is found to be false. No NullPointerException occurs. The overall condition is false, so the else branch executes and prints "short". Option C is the classic trap for students who are unaware that `&&` stops evaluation as soon as the left side is false.

temp = 72. The first condition (72 >= 90) is false, so the second condition (72 >= 70) is evaluated — it is true, so 'warm' is printed. In an if-else-if chain at most one branch executes, so the else block is skipped entirely. D is wrong because once a branch fires, subsequent branches are bypassed.

Without curly braces, only the first statement belongs to the if body. n = 5 is odd, so n % 2 == 0 is false and 'even ' is not printed. The second System.out.print("done") is outside the if block and executes unconditionally, printing 'done'. A is the result a student gets by mistakenly treating both statements as inside the if.

options.label: D
options.text: The code produces a compile-time error

explanation: The && operator short-circuits: because the left operand x != 0 evaluates to false, Java never evaluates y / x. No ArithmeticException occurs. The overall condition is false, so the else branch prints 'not greater'. C is the classic distractor for students who do not know about short-circuit evaluation.

The dangling else pairs with the nearest unmatched if — here that is 'if (val > 200)', not 'if (val > 100)'. Because val = 50 makes the outer condition (val > 100) false, the entire body of the outer if, including the inner if-else, is never entered. Nothing is printed. B is the common mistake made by students who assume the else belongs to the outer if.

Each `new String(...)` call allocates a distinct object in memory. The == operator compares references, so s1 == s2 is false because they point to two different objects. The .equals() method compares character content, so s1.equals(s2) is true because both strings contain "hello". C is wrong because == fails on separate objects; D is wrong because .equals() succeeds on equal content.

These are three independent if statements, not an if-else-if chain, so multiple conditions can fire. 15 % 3 == 0 is true (result becomes 1). 15 % 5 == 0 is true (result becomes 3). 15 % 7 equals 1, not 0, so the third condition is false and result stays 3. D (7 = 1+2+4) would require all three conditions to fire, but 15 is not divisible by 7.
The first condition is true, so `y` becomes 10. The next two conditions are false, so `x` remains 7 and `y` remains 10.
Only `bonus = true;` is controlled by the first `if`, so `points += 2;` always runs. Since `bonus` is still false, the second `if` body does not run.
